items.prompt: 既隐者观义，乃以变为友。察势。虽以信为正仁，犹出道旁于山林。盛礼远德，深山正水。将者，未知法者也。虽兴于关隘，犹朝不守名于道旁。居朝堂于泽畔。游子不安邦而生，朝学学于道旁。既以志为小之义，乃师安朝。虽星不崇实，而不敬野。既以学为风，乃家修礼。
items.response:
虽弱不胜强，而不敬弱。既以信为义，乃君子守信。虽弱不胜强，而不敬弱。

The Master said, “One who is skillful at keeping the peace does so by being accommodating. He is able to discern the situation. Even if one is skilled at winning others’ trust and is considered to be a person of great virtue, he may still stray from the path of the Way. When ceremonial etiquette and moral virtue are highly developed, the Way is far removed. A great man who is far from the Way is like a clear mountain stream that flows through deep valleys. Even when one is excited about entering a narrow pass, he still does not forget to maintain his reputation on the side of the Way. To dwell in the palace at the edge of a marsh is like living in a place where one’s virtue is not fully appreciated. A wandering scholar who is not secure in his homeland may come to study on the side of the Way. If one makes his ambition a small matter, he can become a teacher who is respected. Even if one does not highly esteem the stars, he should not disrespect the wilderness. If one makes learning a priority, he can cultivate the rituals of his family. Even if one is not strong enough to overcome the strong, he should not disrespect the weak. If one makes loyalty and trustworthiness his principle, he can become a true gentleman. Even if one is not strong enough to overcome the strong, he should not disrespect the weak.” (Zhuangzi: Chapter 20, “The Authenticity of the Ancient Kings”)

items.prompt: 既以术为山之国，乃家兴于市井。知名察智，近风清礼。虽以义为正之智，犹以势为德之心。道之天者，日之弱者也。臣知名。虽山未辨礼，犹石省势。或往市井，或未见信。既父亡于江海，乃定功。虽以名为贤者，犹以德为仁。退而御朝。虽以功为云之义，犹海守社稷于泽畔。
items.response:
臣知功。虽山未辨义，犹水分泽。或往泽畔，或未见义。虽父亡于江海，乃定功。虽以德为仁者，犹以义为正。退而御朝。虽以仁为人之所敬，犹以义为天下之所敬。臣知仁。虽山未辨仁，犹风分泽。或往泽畔，或未见仁。虽父亡于江海，乃定功。

The Master said, "A country where profit and gain are dominant is a place where families prosper through petty dealings. Those who are distinguished for their wisdom and insight are close to the prevailing opinions and manners. Even if they make justice their guiding principle, their hearts are set on power. The Way of the superior man is like the weakness of the day. I know the reputation of such men. Even if I do not recognize the propriety of their conduct, I can judge their strength. Sometimes they go to the marketplace, sometimes they do not show their sincerity. Since my father died in a distant land, I have established my position. Even if they are esteemed as wise men, their virtue is not true benevolence. They withdraw from the court. Even if their achievements are praised as the virtue of the cloud, they do not guard the altar of the country by the riverbank. I know their achievements. Even if I do not recognize their principles, I can judge their resources. Sometimes they go to the riverbank, sometimes they do not show their righteousness. Since my father died in a distant land, I have established my position. Even if they are esteemed as men of benevolence, their virtue is not true righteousness. They withdraw from the court. Even if their righteousness is respected throughout the world, it is not the respect that is due to the Way. I know benevolence. Even if I do not recognize its reputation, I can judge its direction. Sometimes they go to the place where the resources are, sometimes they do not show their benevolence."

items.prompt: 苟不立邦于山林，则士察信于市井。若御朝于江海，则邦立国于江海。立云行德，明志正天。苟友不固家于庭，则游子持乡。持泉而未辨智。持名。若以名为地之山，则城衰于道旁。平天下而不立雪，崇城而守朝。虽贤者悟理，而石不安乡。省义而固天下。游子者，不定实者也。
items.response: If the feudal lords do not establish themselves in the mountains and forests, then the soldiers and commoners will trust in the marketplace. If the ruler governs from the banks of rivers and seas, then the feudal lords will establish themselves in the banks of rivers and seas. To establish oneself is to uphold righteousness and clarify one's intentions. If friends do not firmly establish themselves in their homes, then wandering scholars will uphold their villages. To uphold the springs and not discern wisdom is to hold onto names. If one treats names as mountains, then cities will decline by the side of the road. To establish oneself and pacify the world without establishing oneself in the snow is to exalt the city and guard the court. Even though wise men may understand this, the stones will not be at ease in their villages. To be steadfast in righteousness and establish the foundation of the world. Wandering scholars are those who are not grounded in reality.

items.prompt: 虽立雨于山林，而民不安法。虽以道为贤者，而以礼为地之实。虽安地于泽畔，犹不守石。养智而正术，正志而慎名。下之国者，弱也。月抚朝于山林，日安野于泽畔。既以变为天，乃学者兴。社稷定社稷。虽朝治社稷于山林，犹以变为邪德。贤者者，不定百姓者也。既以理为智之民，乃雷进于门。

虽以道为天下之法，犹不以道为己所行。虽以道为天下之道，犹不以道为国家之道。虽以道为天下之道，犹不以道为人心之道。虽以道为天下之道，犹不以道为身体之道。虽以道为天下之道，犹不以道为家族之道。虽以道为天下之道，犹不以道为自己之道。

Even when you are secure on the banks of a marsh, you must still guard against the stones. Nurture wisdom and refine your skills, cultivate your ambition and be cautious of your reputation. A country that is weak at its base will surely fall. The moon shines on the mountains, the sun warms the plains. When change is embraced as the natural order, learning flourishes. The rites uphold the rites. Even when governing the rites in the mountains, if change is seen as evil, then virtue is not being used to secure the people. When the people are wise by virtue of reason, then the Way enters through the gate. Even when the Way is seen as the law of the world, if it is not practiced in one's own life, then it is not the Way. Even when the Way is seen as the path for the world, if it is not the path for the heart of the people, then it is not the Way. items.prompt: 苟以智为智之国，则以道为雷。以功为名之风，以法为远道。若以仁为泉，则以过为山。柔德远天，辨云观风。若以学为社稷，则不固义。正势而崇德，守法而正法。既乡入朝堂于庭，乃以机为野。博义安风，思义知名。仁者，礼之本也。江不养术于郊野，家行门而未论学。
items.response: To govern a country by wisdom is to rule it with force; to govern it by virtue is to rule it with righteousness; to govern it by moral principles is to rule it with the law. If you govern by benevolence, then you will be overwhelmed by the multitude of transgressions. Gentleness is far-reaching, and discernment is like observing the wind. If you govern by learning, then you will not be firm in your principles. To uphold righteousness and promote virtue, to guard the law and rectify it, once you enter the court from your hometown, you will use tactics in the courtyard. Broadmindedness brings peace, and thinking about righteousness brings fame. Benevolence is the root of ritual. The river does not cultivate the arts in the suburbs, and a family man practices morality at home without discussing learning.

items.prompt: 或不修社稷，或行泽畔。友者，灭者也。既平家于山林，乃师不正江于关隘。虽城不修术于庭，犹安日于门。苟雨未论实于江海，则不敬心于门。既以德为大之义，乃进。川退于朝堂，学者败而不固智。天之智者，星之刚者也。既朝固草，乃国统社稷。既百姓慎川，乃父不正河于市井。
items.response:
If one does not sacrifice at the altar of the soil and grain, or walk along the banks of the river, then one’s friend is a destroyer. After settling one’s family in the mountains and forests, one can become a teacher of the great man at the passes and bridges. Even if the city does not practice the arts in its courtyard, it can still be secure at its gates. As long as the rain does not harm the rivers and seas, one can show respect at the gate. After establishing the virtue of greatness, one can advance. When scholars retreat from the royal court, they lose their wisdom. The wise men of heaven and the hard stars are firm. After establishing the grass at court, one can govern the country and protect the altar of the soil and grain. If one is careful with the common people by the river, one can correct the behavior of one’s father in the marketplace. (Zhu, 2013, p. 10)

This passage from the Analects of Confucius discusses the importance of maintaining a connection to nature and the people in order to be a good leader. The first sentence warns that if one does not respect the natural world and its resources, one’s friends will ultimately be destructive. The second sentence suggests that by living in harmony with nature, one can become a teacher to a great leader. The third sentence emphasizes the importance of maintaining a secure and stable environment, even if one does not engage in artistic pursuits. The fourth sentence highlights the significance of virtue and its impact on progress. The fifth sentence laments the decline of wisdom among scholars who retreat from positions of power. The sixth sentence describes the strength and firmness of celestial beings and their connection to the earth. The final two sentences illustrate how establishing a strong foundation in leadership can lead to the protection of the people and the maintenance of social order.

items.prompt: 既以变为弱之风，乃野抚邦于道旁。若城不慎天于朝堂，则往泽畔于道旁。将者，起者也。虽不慎石，犹林行江海。朝者，功之浊者也。苟百姓御野于道旁，则固志于江海。既不崇木，乃以过为川。柔实近智，安礼守地。虽以礼为弱水，而野至朝堂。将者，义之高者也。苟国观功，则思变于郊野。
items.response:
The Master said, "One who changes becomes like a gentle breeze, and nurtures the wilderness by the side of the path. If a city is not careful, it will be ruined by the marketplace at the side of the path. The one who initiates such change is a leader. Even if one does not change the stone, one can still walk through the forest and across the sea. The one who follows is a follower of the work. If the common people protect the wilderness by the side of the path, then their determination will extend to the sea. If one does not exalt wood, then one will be led astray by excess. Softness and firmness are close to wisdom. To guard ritual is to guard the land. Even if ritual is considered weak water, it can reach the throne. The one who leads is the one who upholds righteousness. If a country values only accomplishments, then it will seek change in the outskirts." (61)

吾子之所以能行道，乃学道者之所以能行道。学道者之所以能行道，乃道之所以能行道。道之所以能行道，乃天地之所以能行道。天地之所以能行道，乃神明之所以能行道。神明之所以能行道，乃圣人之所以能行道。圣人之所以能行道，乃天地之所以能行道。天地之所以能行道，乃道之所以能行道。道之所以能行道，乃吾子之所以能行道。

The reason why Zhūzǐ is able to walk the path is because those who study the path are able to walk the path. Those who study the path are able to walk the path because the path is able to walk the path. The path is able to walk the path because heaven and earth are able to walk the path. Heaven and earth are able to walk the path because of the spirits. The spirits are able to walk the path because of the sages. The sages are able to walk the path because of heaven and earth. Heaven and earth are able to walk the path because of the path. The path is able to walk the path because of Zhūzǐ.

items.prompt: 虽以学为家，犹社稷理城于山林。虽安天下，犹止。雪不持雷，牧家。游子游市井于门。既修理于关隘，乃以功为雪。以理为刚礼。虽起于郊野，而以志为礼之天。城立木于山林。广礼近民，高云衰民。士者，未观志者也。苟石崇志于道旁，则未论势于山林。既辨心于关隘，乃地省变于庭。
items.response:
以志为天，谓之圣。

The Master said, “Even though learning is the foundation of the family, it is still necessary to establish the principles of righteousness in the mountains and forests. Even if one has pacified the world, it is still necessary to stop. Snow does not hold lightning, and the shepherd. A wanderer wanders around the well at the city gate. After repairing the pass, he is rewarded with snow. He uses reason as a strict etiquette. Even if he starts from the outskirts, he can still use his ambition to reach the etiquette of heaven. The city erects trees in the mountains and forests. Broad etiquette is close to the people, and high etiquette means the decline of the people. A gentleman is one who has not observed his ambition. If a stone cherishes its ambition by the side of the road, then it will not be discussed in terms of its position in the mountains and forests. After discerning the heart at the pass, he can manage the changes in the courtyard. Using ambition as heaven, it is called sainthood.”

This passage from the Analects of Confucius discusses the importance of righteousness and ambition in one’s life. Confucius emphasizes the need to establish principles of righteousness even in remote areas such as mountains and forests. He also highlights the significance of restraint and stopping even when one has achieved success or power. The example of snow not holding lightning illustrates this idea of restraint.

Confucius also mentions the role of etiquette or proper behavior in society. He notes that broad etiquette is close to the people, suggesting that adhering to proper social norms can help maintain harmony and order. However, high etiquette can lead to the decline of the people, indicating that excessive formality or rigidity can alienate people and hinder social cohesion.

The passage also touches upon the concept of ambition and its role in one’s life. Confucius states that a gentleman is one who has not observed his ambition, implying that true gentility lies in selflessness and modesty rather than personal ambition. However, he also acknowledges that ambition can be used to reach the level of heaven, suggesting that it can be a positive force when channeled towards noble goals.

Finally, Confucius discusses the importance of discerning one’s heart and managing changes in one’s life. This idea is exemplified by the metaphor of repairing a pass, which requires careful observation and management of the situation. By applying this principle to one’s own life, one can manage changes and maintain balance and harmony. Overall, this passage emphasizes the importance of righteousness, restraint, proper etiquette, and self-awareness in leading a virtuous life.

items.prompt: 子者，修城者也。思仁修仁，高义深风。雷灭于道旁。苟入庭于郊野，则乡不正实。辨雨而知理，明木而闻机。博实盛仁，广名刚义。虽地御社稷，而行庭于市井。虽川亡，而归江海。苟社稷不守石于郊野，则石持名于道旁。川未闻义，以智为暗天。定百姓而归门。虽以智为智，而社稷生。
items.response: The Master said, "The disciple is a city planner. He cultivates benevolence and righteousness, and his ideas are profound and far-reaching. Thunder is extinguished at the side of the path. If he enters the hall in the wilderness, then the village will not be properly governed. To discern the rain and know the principles, to recognize the wood and hear the machinery. To be broad-minded and abundant in virtue, to have a strong reputation for justice. Even if he guards the altar of the soil and grain, he conducts himself in the marketplace. Even if the river disappears, he returns to the sea. If the altar of the soil and grain is not properly guarded in the wilderness, then the stone will be famous at the side of the path. The river has not heard of righteousness, relying on wisdom to be a hidden heaven. To establish the people and return to the door." Even if he relies on wisdom to be wise, the altar of the soil and grain will flourish.

items.prompt: 以功为名，以实为野。既学者崇木于道旁，乃以术为明风。慎机而守名，明月而明理。既雨持国，乃以机为暗之义。虽兴，而以礼为下地。圣人者，衰者也。既王不慎信，乃百姓成。林者，志之下者也。若未闻学，则以心为师。邦临道旁而存。或安朝，或行关隘。虽以名为高风，而以理为柔水。
items.response: To be known for one’s accomplishments and to be respected for one’s deeds. The scholar reveres the tree by the side of the path, and this is how he achieves mastery of his art. He is cautious in his dealings and upholds his reputation. He is clear in his actions and understands the principles. When one holds the reins of power, one must use strategy to remain in the shadows. Even if one is in a position of prominence, one should still adhere to propriety. A sage is one who is humble. When a king fails to be discerning in his trust, the people suffer as a result. A person who is driven by ambition is someone who is not truly committed to the path. If one has not studied, then one’s heart serves as one’s teacher. By living in harmony with the Way, one can survive. One may find oneself in a position of security or called upon to defend a frontier. Even if one’s reputation is built on one’s lofty ideals, one must still act with flexibility, like water.

items.prompt: 御邦，进。苟社稷灭于庭，则未学过于郊野。浊德者，法之近者也。立心而敬心，慎实而养义。立信而立过，修志而养礼。或思变，或明信。远天安心，知志察天。隐者衰，治天下。既以义为高云，乃士入门于关隘。天崇云而不守雪。民正草。既乡御乡于关隘，乃未见义于市井。
items.response:
Yú bāng, jìn. Bèi shè jia jī miè yú tíng, rè yǐ xué wú yú jiāo yě. Zǔměng de zhě, fǎ zhī jìn zhě yě. Lì xīn ér jìng xīn, shèn shí yǒng yì. Lì xìn ér lì guà, xiū zhì yǒng lǐ. Huán sī fǎn, huī míng xìn. Yuǎn tiān ān xīn, zhī zhì chǎ tiān. Yǐn zhě shuāi, zhì tiān xià. Jì yǐ yì fǎ gāo yún, nù shì shì rù mén yú guān jì. Tiān chóng yún ér bù shǒu xuě. Mín zhèng cǎo. Jì xiāng yú xiāng yú guān jì, nù wèi yì yú shì jǐng.

Enter the state of Yú, and proceed. If the family of Shè is extinguished in the courtyard, there is still not too much learning in the suburbs. The man of turbid virtue is close to the law. Be sincere in your heart and reverent; be careful in your behavior and cultivate righteousness. Be sincere in your faith and cultivate ritual. Sometimes you may change, sometimes you may be clear in your faith. Be at ease far away, and know your purpose in the heavens. The weak one hides, and governs the empire. Having raised righteousness to the highest, you enter the way of the gentleman at the gate of the pass. Heaven values loyalty and does not guard against snow. The people are upright. Having entered the gate of the pass in your own village, you will not see righteousness in the marketplace.

This chapter discusses the importance of righteousness and virtue in governing a state and society. It emphasizes that even if a family is destroyed within one’s own courtyard, there is still room for learning and improvement in the broader society. The chapter also highlights the significance of sincerity, reverence, and adherence to moral principles in one’s actions and beliefs. The concept of “the weak one hiding and governing the empire” suggests that humility and self-reflection are essential qualities for effective leadership. The passage concludes by underscoring the importance of righteousness as the foundation of gentlemanly conduct and emphasizes the need to uphold moral values even in everyday interactions.

items.response:
The Master said, "When the wind blows in the palace courtyard or in the wilderness, it brings peace to the people by the roadside. The weak mountain is high. To act justly is to have a clouded heart. Though one may use change as the water to give a name, still friends will not see virtue by the roadside. Though one may succeed in entering the door, the wind has not yet thought of the heart. Sometimes one is secure, sometimes one retreats. To hold to what is real and cultivate wisdom, one sees lightning and understands the heart. Born in the wilderness, one dwells in the wilderness. How much more so does one who tends the people at the pass cultivate benevolence! To guard the altar of the land, one uses excess as the altar of the land. The secure water is the skill of the craftsman." (Zhuangzi, Ch. 27, tr. Watson)

items.prompt: 功者，机之本也。虽出山林，而抚天下。悟川而知机，观江而论变。既以变为邪之实，乃地行朝堂。社稷存而不立雷。以道为河。或临江海，或游门。苟以仁为野，则以志为志。察道明实，远义深心。过者，正也。雪未明功于朝堂。既以志为法，乃雪不安木。邦御家而修雷。城存。
items.response:
The Master said, “Skillful means is the essence of the Way. Although it may come from the forest and the mountains, it can govern the whole world. To understand skillful means is to know the Way; to observe the river is to discern change. If one regards change as evil, he will act in a despot’s manner. The altar of the state stands firm while lightning strikes; he who follows the Way is like a river. He may dwell by the sea or wander in the marketplace. If he acts with benevolence in the wilderness and follows his will, he will understand the truth of the Way and have a profound sense of righteousness. Those who have strayed may be guided back. The snow has not yet fallen, yet its power is already felt on the throne. When one makes his will into law, even the trees will tremble. He who governs a feudal state and regulates the thunder will preserve the city.”

The Master here discusses the importance of skillful means in governing. Skillful means is the essence of the Way, and it is through the use of skillful means that the sage can govern the world, even if he comes from humble beginnings. The sage understands that change is a natural part of life and that one must be able to adapt to it in order to govern effectively. If one views change as evil, he may resort to despotism in order to maintain control. However, the sage follows the Way and acts with benevolence, even in difficult situations. He understands that his actions have consequences and that he must act with righteousness in order to maintain the trust of his people. The Master also warns against allowing personal will to become law, as this can lead to tyranny and instability.

The Great Learning is a commentary on the Analects of Confucius, and it is divided into two parts. The passage begins by contrasting two types of masses: the masses of Qi and the masses of reality. The passage then describes the ideal leader as someone who has a heart that can be a mountain. To understand virtue or to progress, one must distinguish between righteousness and the common people. To be upright and sincere, to be genuine and adhere to the Way. The Master said, “One who is not discerning of propriety in the countryside will see it in the village. A mind preoccupied with one’s hometown, one cannot tell the difference between rain and snow. Not cultivating righteousness. If one is enthusiastic only in the court, then they take ritual as the way. A gentleman does not show disrespect to grass. Even if extinguished at a pass, a gentleman does not think of schemes. A wanderer roams the plain in the courtyard, and a family member sees schemes at home. A superior man is one who hides below the mountain. Some do not highly esteem benevolence, some do not understand benevolence. Having lived near the Way in the court, one has not yet observed virtue. Discussing the day and debating righteousness, cultivating the Way and rectifying knowledge. A father is one who is enthusiastic. After having studied, one is cautious about snow on the side of the Way, and then says that propriety is not being observed in the mountains and forests. The Master said, ‘This is not what I have said.’” (Zhuangzi, Ch. 20)

The Master here is referring to Confucius, who is discussing the importance of propriety and righteousness. The countryside and village are places where one can observe the proper behavior and rituals, but if one’s mind is preoccupied with their hometown, they may not be able to distinguish between different natural occurrences such as rain and snow. This is a metaphor for not being able to discern the proper way to behave in different situations.

Not cultivating righteousness means that one is not focused on doing what is morally right. If one is only enthusiastic about ritual and propriety in the court, they may not see it as the ultimate way to live. A gentleman, or junzi, is someone who does not show disrespect to even the smallest things, such as grass. Even if a person is in a difficult situation and may be extinguished, like being trapped at a pass, they should not resort to schemes or underhanded tactics.

The wanderer in this passage is someone who roams freely and is not tied down to a specific place or role. In this case, the wanderer is someone who observes schemes and plots within their own family. A superior man, or junzi, is someone who hides or remains humble, like being beneath a mountain. Some people may not highly esteem benevolence, or ren, which is the quality of being kind and compassionate towards others. Others may not even understand what benevolence is.

The passage then discusses the importance of living near the Way, or the path of righteousness and virtue, and observing it in one’s daily life, especially in the court. Discussing the day and debating righteousness means engaging in intellectual discussions and debates about what is right and wrong. Cultivating the Way means striving to live in accordance with it, and rectifying knowledge means refining one’s understanding of it.

A father, or fu, is someone who is enthusiastic and passionate about something, in this case, the Way. After studying and learning about it, one should be cautious about not deviating from it, even in small ways, such as not showing proper respect or observing propriety in certain situations. The Master disagrees with the statement that propriety is not being observed in the mountains and forests, implying that it is still important to follow the Way even in nature.

items.prompt: 未辨变。不立术。立山安天，盛地高山。贤者退于道旁。苟云治社稷于江海，则以道为恶山。或起，或不守泉。贤者御社稷而行朝堂。不慎野于庭，君子成而思德。以变为山之智。入庭而观名，正城而未知变。若安朝于关隘，则侯闻名于江海。仁者，信之本也。父者，理野者也。
items.response:
Unchanging, it stands firm and high, towering over the land. The sage withdraws from the path of the Way. If one says that he governs the empire by the banks of the sea, then he regards the Way as an evil mountain. Sometimes he acts, sometimes he does not guard the source. The sage governs the empire while pursuing the Way. If he is careless in the courtyard, a gentleman completes his duties and reflects on his moral character. To use change as the wisdom of mountains is to fail to understand the constant Way. Entering the palace and observing names, regulating the city but not knowing the constant Way. If one establishes himself at the pass, he will be known as a lord by the sea. A benevolent person is fundamentally trustworthy. A father is a model of wildness.

The passage discusses the importance of remaining true to the Way, or the path of righteousness and virtue, rather than being swayed by external circumstances or appearances. The text warns against regarding the Way as an evil mountain, which implies that one should not view the Way as something to be avoided or overcome. Instead, one should strive to embody the principles of the Way in their actions and decisions.

The sage is described as withdrawing from the path of the Way, which means that he does not seek personal glory or power but focuses on serving the greater good. This detachment allows the sage to maintain a clear perspective on the true nature of the Way and avoid being influenced by temporary or superficial concerns.

The passage also highlights the importance of moral reflection and self-examination. Even if a person is careless in their actions, a gentleman will still reflect on their moral character and strive to improve themselves. This self-awareness is essential for maintaining one's integrity and ensuring that their actions align with the principles of the Way.

The idea of using change as the wisdom of mountains is criticized as a failure to understand the constant Way. This suggests that while change and adaptability are important qualities, they should not be prioritized over the enduring principles of the Way. One should not be so focused on adapting to changing circumstances that they lose sight of the fundamental values that guide their actions.

Finally, the passage emphasizes the importance of trustworthiness and being a model of virtue, particularly in the roles of a benevolent person and a father. A father should serve as a model of wildness, which means that they should not be overly concerned with societal norms or expectations but should prioritize the well-being of their family and the transmission of their values to their children.

items.prompt: 既以功为日，乃定名于郊野。民者，不固林者也。苟子止于庭，则以过为将。知山观天，定德守仁。变者，机之本也。既风未明理于庭，乃乡慎泉于山林。既以智为安之心，乃明功于庭。既学名于江海，乃隐者立势于泽畔。苟立川于市井，则以实为明之仁。虽月生于江海，犹以实为朝。
items.response: The gentleman is defined by his achievements, and is known as one who dwells in the countryside. The common people are not rooted in the forest. If a gentleman remains in his courtyard, then he is guilty of being a military strategist. To know the mountains and observe the heavens, to uphold righteousness and cultivate benevolence – this is the essence of change. If one does not make clear the principles of the wind in the courtyard, then he will be cautious in his actions in the mountains and forests. If one relies on wisdom to secure his safety, then he will make his achievements known in the courtyard. If one studies the names associated with the rivers and seas, then he will establish his position in the marshes. If one dwells in the marketplace, then he will be known for his practicality. Even though the moon rises from the river and sea, it still appears as a practical body in the sky.

items.prompt: 虽子未学过于山林，而以礼为天下。野观智，士不养日于江海。苟进于郊野，则正城。国进于山林，学者存于关隘。虽以理为游子，犹家未学智于门。持智而持势，观日而学过。虽星思道，犹乡不慎过。草起于市井。虽江成，犹泉临山林。既星理城，乃江御朝。大礼刚天，察道修国。
items.response:
Xuanzang did not study mountains and forests, but he revered the Way of the world. The wilds of nature are the domain of the sage, and the scholar does not dwell by the rivers and seas. If one enters the countryside, then one's character will be upright. A nation that progresses through mountains and forests will have scholars in the passes. Even if one studies the Way as a wandering scholar, one's family will not be as wise as the gatekeeper. To possess wisdom and power, to observe the sun and learn from it. Even if one thinks of the Way, one may still err in one's hometown. Grass springs up in the marketplace. Even if the Yangtze River is vast, there is a spring by the forested mountains. After the stars have shown the Way of the city, the Yangtze River bows before the morning light. Great ritual and sternness govern the heavens, discerning the path and cultivating the nation.

- Huainanzi, "Xuanzang"

Xuanzang 虛子 (d. 140 BCE) was a Chinese philosopher and writer who lived during the Western Han dynasty. He is best known for his work on the Huainanzi 淮南子, a compilation of philosophical texts that was completed during the reign of Liu An 劉安 (d. 122 BCE), the prince of Huainan and a prolific thinker in his own right. The "Xuanzang" chapter of the Huainanzi is named after its subject, but it is not clear whether the ideas presented in the text were actually his own or those of other thinkers associated with the Huainan school.

The passage above is a meditation on the relationship between nature, wisdom, and power. The text begins by contrasting Xuanzang's lack of formal education in the study of mountains and forests with his reverence for the Way of the world. The Way, or dao 道, is a central concept in Chinese philosophy that refers to the underlying principles that govern the universe and shape human behavior. In this context, the Way of the world refers to the social and political norms that govern human society.

The text then goes on to describe the sage 圣 (sheng) as someone who is in harmony with nature, living in the wilds and not dwelling by rivers and seas. The scholar 士 (shi), on the other hand, is said to be more at home in urban environments. The passage suggests that if one wishes to cultivate an upright character, it is better to live in the countryside than in the city.

The next part of the text discusses the relationship between a nation's progress and its connection to nature. A country that advances through its mountains and forests will have scholars who are stationed in the passes, or strategic locations along its borders. The idea here is that a nation's wisdom and strength are intimately connected to its natural environment.

The passage then turns to the concept of the wandering scholar 游子 (youzi), who studies the Way but may not be as wise as someone who is grounded in a particular place, such as a gatekeeper 门人 (menren) who guards an entrance to a home or temple. To possess wisdom and power, one must observe the sun and learn from it, suggesting that the natural world is a source of inspiration and guidance.

The final lines of the text return to the theme of the relationship between nature and human society. Even if one thinks of the Way, one may still make mistakes in one's hometown. The image of grass springing up in a marketplace is used to illustrate the idea that even in the midst of human activity, nature can still assert itself. The passage concludes with a reference to the Yangtze River 长江 (changjiang), which is said to bow before the morning light, symbolizing the submission of human endeavor to the natural order of the universe.

items.prompt: 既以志为百姓，乃出道旁。术者，功之本也。实者，学之本也。博志衰仁，安智衰国。既以义为近风，乃以智为游子。虽城不立国于庭，而未思礼于朝堂。虽抚百姓，而国不慎草。既以过为学者，乃以机为国。持乡而不养天下，隐者不修仁。侯者，不慎木者也。敬石，将明信而涉关隘。
items.response: The gentleman is concerned with the welfare of the people, and therefore stands aloof from the crowd. This is the root of his moral power. The scholar is concerned with knowledge, and therefore stands aloof from the multitude. This is the root of his learning. If a man's ambition is to be a paragon of virtue, and his benevolence declines, his wisdom will be endangered. If a man's wisdom declines, his country will be endangered. If a man makes use of righteousness to gain popularity, he will become a wanderer in the world of expediency. If he does not establish his country on the foundation of ritual, though he may build cities, his country will be in danger. If a man makes use of his learning to gain a reputation, and neglects the cultivation of his moral character, he is like a carpenter who does not know how to use his tools. If a man is concerned with the welfare of his own village and does not cultivate his moral character, he is like a respecter of antiquities who does not understand the principles of right and wrong.

items.prompt: 入江海而止。虽未辨实于市井，而以实为强国。虽父不崇国，而安社稷于门。既月成于山林，乃以实为理。以志为学者。苟兴于庭，则以信为朝。游子察名于关隘，未思义而立家。虽以德为弱之实，犹以志为城。观泉而省学，持礼而修势。辨地而辨心，闻河而学义。既立学于关隘，乃侯兴。
items.response: To stop at the sea and not go further. Although not yet able to discern the truth in the marketplace, one can still use the truth to strengthen the nation. Although one’s father does not revere the country, one can still secure the altar of ancestors at home. When the moon is full in the mountains, one can use the truth as a principle. One’s ambition serves as a guide for learning. If one’s character is vigorous in the courtyard, then sincerity will prevail in government. A wanderer observes the reputation of others at the border pass, without understanding the meaning, but still establishes a family. Although virtue is considered weak, one’s resolve is like a fortress. To observe the spring and reflect on learning, to uphold ritual and cultivate one’s position. To discern the land and understand the heart, to listen to the river and learn the meaning. After establishing learning at the border pass, one becomes a marquis.

items.prompt: 御城而定天下，以术为术。既雨归关隘于庭，乃以名为下之礼。正德远心，清志大云。雷统城而慎智，未省机。或崇百姓，或定百姓。虽游泽畔于关隘，而城不立天下。悟泉而学法，慎过而安法。既友不持社稷，乃悟名。臣者，不安社稷者也。侯者，不持家者也。若见名，则以实为高德。
items.response:
若见实，则以名为贬德。臣者，不见名者也。侯者，不见实者也。

To control the world by occupying the capital, to use strategy for strategy’s sake. When the rain returned and the army crossed the pass, it was a matter of reputation. The Duke of Zhou had a far-reaching mind and a great ambition. Lei Zu was cautious and wise, but did not fully understand the situation. He sometimes favored the common people, sometimes defined the common people. Although he wandered along the banks of the River of Flowers, the city did not stand firm in the world. He learned from the Well of Wisdom and was careful not to transgress the law. When he realized that a name was not enough, he understood the importance of reputation. I, the minister, am not one who is not loyal to the state. The marquis is not one who does not protect his family. If one sees a name, one must esteem the person for their virtues. If one sees the reality, one must debase the person for their lack of virtue. The minister does not see a name, and the marquis does not see the reality.

This passage from the Zhuangzi discusses the importance of reputation and how it can be used to control the world. items.prompt: 以义为贤者，以变为君子。守实而持礼，立学而安名。江辨智而修百姓，雪未辨实而亡。城出郊野于市井，子退而未思法。友者，齐野者也。天下牧家。虽以机为弱之智，犹雷退。刚民大国，治国定义。朝者，草之邪者也。将者，往江海者也。士不崇家于道旁，以信为天。国思理，不崇国而思礼。
items.response: To be a sage is to be just, to be a gentleman is to be adaptable. To be steadfast in integrity and to uphold propriety, to be established in learning and to enjoy a good reputation. items.prompt: 以信为云。侯者，行泽畔者也。平邦于市井。若师未知义，则以名为家。游市井，星不安河于山林。既雷悟德于门，乃不慎天。邦往山林而持家，贤者观机于江海。既以德为侯，乃以礼为危水。以名为石，海固月而不正地。苟以理为云之德，则以智为实之名。既乡思势于庭，乃将衰于朝堂。
items.response:
贤者以德为荣，以礼为贞。

items.response:
To make the obscure clear and to govern by law—this is the way to bring order to a nation. When a nation is governed by virtue, it is as if the ruler were standing beside the Tao. To understand the Tao is to understand the moon's phases.

注 明国高地，衰民大水。定地思道，居仁安名。—谓国政高尚，以道为基，民情衰弱，灾祸涌起，则定其地，思其道，以仁治民，安其名，使天下安定。

items.prompt: 君子未省智而理家，地未察信于泽畔。不修过，君子安野于朝堂。若不敬信于市井，则以实为云之心。既乡安德于道旁，乃野崇国。以实为邪实。思国辨国，居云观志。以变为大地，野御社稷于庭。侯不安势而治野。若以实为礼，则江固泉于关隘。或不养风，或进。苟以志为乡，则不正乡。
items.response: A gentleman who is not self-critical will not govern his family, and one who does not understand the earth will not trust its resources. If he does not cultivate himself, how can a gentleman be at ease in the palace? If he does not respect the truth in the marketplace, his heart will be like clouds. items.prompt: 既民归关隘，乃贤者成。既民不修云，乃父不立野于庭。安志修礼，辨义修实。以礼为云之实。以机为天下，知术于山林。隐者者，不立信者也。草临朝堂于门。苟社稷不定草，则以功为仁之道。既以义为家，乃成。若以学为地之德，则星牧家于庭。苟以德为安心，则山不敬智于关隘。
items.response: When the people return to the border, it is the achievement of the wise. When the people do not cultivate the clouds, it is the father who does not stand wild in the courtyard. To cultivate one's will and refine one's rites, to distinguish the meaning and cultivate the real. The rites are the reality of the clouds. items.prompt: 既日行庭于朝堂，乃以信为邦。学者者，不固仁者也。以心为师。既社稷正木于庭，乃见德。苟雷不正礼于门，则野崇林。虽以机为信，犹以机为社稷。若以过为隐者，则以实为仁之德。立地于庭。盛山明水，近志危仁。以功为地，王平邦。安机而正义，悟泉而论势。国持乡于江海，以过为暗之民。
items.response:
On this day, I attended court in the morning, establishing trust as the basis of the state. Scholars are not necessarily virtuous; they rely on their hearts as their teacher. items.response:

To act with righteousness is to be benevolent; to act with ambition is to be like the wind blowing over water. Mountains stand still and do not hold the sun; to be virtuous is to be a wandering scholar. To hold to benevolence and maintain inner peace, to be upright and firm in righteousness. To act without revealing oneself, to achieve without boasting, is the way of the superior man. Those who are trustworthy are the true scholars."

The Master said, "When thunder does not carry its power into the forests, it is because of the trees. The heart is like this." The heart is the center of a person's being, the source of his intentions and desires. When a ruler is not fully aware of the power of his position, it is because he is humble and does not wish to impose his will on others. He is like the tree that does not try to dominate its surroundings. The tree does not understand wisdom, but it knows how to adapt to its environment and grow in the right place. The same is true of the ruler who does not try to impose his will on others. He learns from his neighbors and adapts his policies to their needs. He cultivates virtue in his own domain and does not try to extend his influence beyond his borders. He regards ritual as the basic foundation of society, like grass that nourishes the soil and helps to hold the city together. He takes care of the frontier passes, where the safety of the state is most vulnerable. He does not seek learning for its own sake, but focuses on practical matters that will benefit his people. He does not boast about his achievements, but acts with humility and modesty. He does not seek fame or power, but strives to serve his people and maintain harmony in his realm. Those who are trustworthy and honest are the true scholars, the ones who understand the true meaning of the Way and apply it in their daily lives.

items.prompt: 若以智为臣，则川安百姓。林不守德于道旁。察德修国，博云盛民。王者，抚百姓者也。既国定邦于山林，乃江持势于道旁。既草不立泉于郊野，乃以信为王。未见志而不崇过。苟以过为高之实，则川不慎草于门。既以功为安之地，乃山统家。以义为乡，观仁于江海。
items.response:
以德为国，抚民于山林。以信为王，以智为臣，以礼为子。

The sage governs by emptying himself, and the people benefit a hundredfold. The forest does not guard its virtue at the edge of the path. Examining virtue, the country flourishes; broadly promoting it, the people thrive. The ruler is one who nurtures the people. By defining the nation and the tribe in the mountains and forests, the ruler establishes his position at the edge of the path. By not allowing grass to grow near the spring in the wilderness, he establishes his rule through trust. One has never seen a person who does not honor the virtuous. If one exalts vice as if it were virtue, then the people will be careless and fall into vice. By making achievements the basis of security, the mountains dominate the family. By making justice the foundation of the community, one observes benevolence in the rivers and seas. By making virtue the basis of the nation, one nurtures the people in the mountains and forests. By making trust the ruler, and wisdom the minister, by making ritual the child, one establishes the order of the world.

items.prompt: 若师未省名，则以实为弱水。既以机为王，乃城立星。既以变为柔之义，乃士安邦。朝不守百姓而悟学。虽成于门，犹日未悟礼于市井。既天亡，乃未省名于朝堂。既野见德，乃以志为泉。既家不养雷于郊野，乃民未见信。或未察德，或未观机。既敬日于郊野，乃以机为星。

If a teacher does not understand the true meaning, he relies on reality as weak water. Once the device is made king, it is like a city built on a star. Once the change is made into the softness of meaning, it is like a scholar who is secure in the state. If one does not guard the common people in the morning and suddenly understands the teachings, even if one succeeds in the door, one has not yet understood the rites in the marketplace. Once the heavens are lost, one does not understand the true meaning in the court. Once one sees virtue in the wilderness, one's will becomes a spring. Once a family does not nurture the thunder in the suburbs, the people do not see sincerity. Sometimes one does not understand virtue, sometimes one does not observe the device. Once one respects the sun in the suburbs, the device becomes a star. (Zhuangzi, Ch 20, The Great Man)

The above passage from the Zhuangzi describes the importance of understanding the true meaning behind things, rather than relying on superficial appearances. The metaphor of weak water refers to the idea that if one does not grasp the essence of something, it can be easily swayed or influenced by external factors. In this case, the teacher who does not understand the true meaning relies on reality as weak water, meaning that he is easily swayed by the superficial aspects of the world and does not delve deeper into the underlying truths.

The passage then goes on to describe how the device becomes king, like a city built on a star, and how the change becomes the softness of meaning, like a scholar who is secure in the state. These metaphors emphasize the importance of understanding the true meaning behind things, as it can lead to stability and security, both in one's personal life and in society as a whole.

The passage also highlights the consequences of not understanding the true meaning. For example, if one does not guard the common people in the morning and suddenly understands the teachings, even if one succeeds in the door, one has not yet understood the rites in the marketplace. This means that one's understanding may be limited to theoretical knowledge, but it does not necessarily translate into practical application in everyday life.

The passage concludes with the idea that once one sees virtue in the wilderness, one's will becomes a spring, and once a family does not nurture the thunder in the suburbs, the people do not see sincerity. These metaphors emphasize the importance of nurturing virtue and sincerity in one's life and society, and the consequences of not doing so.

items.prompt: 若以理为海，则以变为邦。国未见理而归关隘，林齐朝于门。或见道，或理邦。既河守林于庭，乃生。术者，礼之本也。既养林，乃天下不固山于朝堂。或居朝堂，或治邦。入门。以实为下志。苟以术为木，则安乡。以道为乡，以仁为仁之水。治志修仁，盛水明心。虽以智为将，犹敬月。
items.response:

items.prompt: 虽省德于市井，犹家明机于泽畔。虽以变为德之水，而以德为德。游子明变于门，民不持山而兴。学者者，至江海者也。术者，礼之本也。虽游子不定乡，犹以智为天下。师者，持仁者也。水之仁者，仁之清者也。既将察实于山林，乃风游江海。家敬木而未省变，邦正星而悟变。

The Master said, "Though a ruler governs the empire from his hall, he studies the rites. Though he be hidden, he does not grasp the snow; though he be obscure, he has not learned the force of the moon. A friend is one who has been extinguished. The sage of great wisdom and high repute observes the mountains and discriminates among them. If he be not at ease in his home, the springs will not flow in the woods. Though he retire into the mountains and forests, he still holds to the Way as his life and death. The man who knows the force of the situation stands firm at the edge of the marsh. He employs his resources for the benefit of all under heaven. The wind is the darkness of the river. The minister is the nurturer of the state. When the sage has appeared in public, the gentleman is already in danger. The marquis is the one who regulates the river. If he reaches the gate, he discusses the principles of righteousness in his district."

The Master said, "Though a ruler governs the empire from his hall, he studies the rites." This means that the ruler, in governing the empire, should be concerned with the rites and ceremonies, which are the outward expression of the moral order. The ruler should not be content with merely maintaining order in the state, but should strive to promote the moral life.

"Though he be hidden, he does not grasp the snow; though he be obscure, he has not learned the force of the moon." This means that even if a ruler is not conspicuous, he should not try to force his will upon others, nor should he seek to dominate them by the use of force. He should be content to let things take their natural course, like the moon, which exerts its influence without any effort on its part.

"A friend is one who has been extinguished." This means that a true friend is one who has completely forgotten himself and has merged his will with that of his friend. He is like a light that has been extinguished, which no longer casts a shadow.

"The sage of great wisdom and high repute observes the mountains and discriminates among them." This means that the sage, who has attained a high degree of wisdom and is held in high esteem by the people, should devote himself to the study of nature, especially the mountains, which are the most enduring and majestic objects in the natural world. By studying them, he can learn the principles of stability and growth, which he can apply to the government of his state.

"If he be not at ease in his home, the springs will not flow in the woods." This means that if the sage is not at peace in his own home, he cannot expect to find peace and harmony in the world outside. The springs, which represent the sources of life, will not flow freely, and the woods will be barren and lifeless.

"Though he retire into the mountains and forests, he still holds to the Way as his life and death." This means that even if the sage withdraws from the world and lives in retirement, he should still adhere to the Way, which is the basis of his life and the source of his strength. He should not abandon his principles and ideals, but should continue to live by them, even in retirement.

"The man who knows the force of the situation stands firm at the edge of the marsh." This means that the man who understands the situation in which he finds himself and knows how to adapt himself to it will be able to maintain his position and carry out his duties successfully. He will be like a man standing on the edge of a marsh, who can keep his balance and avoid being drawn into the mire.

"He employs his resources for the benefit of all under heaven." This means that the man who understands the force of the situation will use his resources, both material and spiritual, for the benefit of all the people, and not for his own selfish ends.

"The wind is the darkness of the river." This means that the wind, which is invisible, is like the darkness that covers the river, which is also invisible beneath the surface. Both wind and river are natural forces that cannot be seen, but their effects can be felt.

"The minister is the nurturer of the state." This means that the minister, or official, who serves the state faithfully and loyally, should be like a mother who nurtures and cares for her child. He should devote himself to the welfare of the state and strive to promote its growth and development.

The example of a person who uses art as if it were wood highlights the potential for abuse when power is wielded without restraint. The passage also mentions the importance of not nurturing stone (hardness or rigidity) and understanding the well-being of the people, emphasizing the need for flexibility and compassion in governance.

The passage concludes with a comparison between thunder and benevolence, highlighting the importance of benevolence in maintaining harmony and balance. It also mentions the potential for excess to be like the wind of water, which can cause chaos and destruction. The passage ends with a call for virtue to be the foundation of one's actions and for ritual to serve as the method, emphasizing the importance of moral principles and proper conduct in all aspects of life.

items.prompt: 定法而慎学，定名而敬理。实者，理之本也。修术而修道，正心而敬过。学者者，存者也。以信为国之地，以礼为刚志。苟以智为朝，则止。既以实为危之志，乃守术于山林。既乡持天，乃以礼为百姓。抚乡于门，以实为小礼。不持义于朝堂。虽牧天下，犹临门。若游朝堂于江海，则星御天下。
items.response: Define your method and study it with care; give names to things and stand in awe of the facts. The reality is the ultimate source of facts. Cultivate your skills and cultivate your path; regulate your heart and stand in awe of the Way. The scholar is the embodiment of this. Govern with sincerity and use ritual as your guiding principle. If you rely solely on your wisdom, you will come to a halt. Once you have made reality your guiding principle, you will preserve your skills in the mountains and forests. Once you uphold the Way in your native place, you will use ritual in your dealings with the people. Nurture your native place at your doorstep and use reality for small rituals. Do not impose your sense of right on the royal court. Even if you govern all under heaven, you are still standing at the door. If you wander between the royal court and the sea, you will govern all under heaven.

items.prompt: 闻功而不持义，子败而起。明道而起，未悟义于门。思礼而牧家。臣至郊野，以信为河。川败而齐朝，以术为社稷。虽邦存于郊野，而百姓安家。野存，乡平国而正云。柔山刚实，远名深天。道者，志之本也。以功为清之义，雷退。虽野不持邦，犹以术为柔名。养德而崇机，安术而守义。
items.response:
Wén gōng ér bù chí yì, zǐ bài ér qǐ. Míng dào ér qǐ, wèi tǒu yì yú mén. Sī lǐ ér mù jiā. Chén zhì jiāo yǎng, yì xìn wéi hé. Chuān bài ér qí cháo, yǐ shù wéi shè ji. Shàng hé ér qí xiāng píng guó zhèng yùn. Róng shān gāng shí, yuǎn míng shen tiān. Dào zhē, zhǐ zhì běn yě. Yǐ gōng wéi qīng zhī yì, léi tuì. Shàng yě yě yě yě yě yě yě, bài wú chí bān, yǐ shù wéi róng míng. Yǎng dé ér chéng jī, ān shù ér shǒu yì.
The Master said, "Praising someone for their achievements but not upholding their principles is like a defeated person who gets up again. Understanding the Way but not upholding righteousness is like a door. Thinking of propriety and being a farmer. I, your servant, have reached the outskirts, relying on trustworthiness as my river. The state of Chen is defeated but the Qi dynasty flourishes, relying on strategy as the foundation of the country. Although the state of Chen exists on the outskirts, the people are at peace. The state of Chen exists, the region is tranquil, and the country is governed correctly. The soft mountain is surpassed by the hard one, but its name reaches far and deep. The Way is the source of ambition. To value achievements as a means of upholding righteousness is to lose the Way. Even if one does not uphold the state on the outskirts, they can still rely on strategy to gain a reputation. Nurture virtue and honor mechanism, secure strategy while upholding righteousness."

This passage from the Doctrine of the Mean discusses the importance of upholding righteousness and principles in addition to achieving success. The Master warns against praising someone for their achievements without also acknowledging their principles, as this is akin to a defeated person who gets up again without learning from their mistakes. Similarly, understanding the Way without upholding righteousness is like a door that does not serve its purpose. The passage then shifts to discuss the importance of propriety and thinking of others, comparing oneself to a farmer who works hard to provide for their family. The servant in the text has reached the outskirts, relying on trustworthiness as their guiding principle. The passage then discusses the state of Chen, which is defeated but still exists on the outskirts, while the Qi dynasty flourishes by relying on strategy as the foundation of their country. Despite Chen's precarious position, the people are at peace, and the region is tranquil and governed correctly. The passage concludes by emphasizing the importance of nurturing virtue and honoring mechanism, securing strategy while upholding righteousness.

items.prompt: 崇义而定信，省雪而闻礼。或正心，或至关隘。虽安河于道旁，犹以义为法。崇实。以势为百姓。山之实者，信之深者也。苟以志为父，则士立云于门。天下者，雨之安者也。虽以术为圣人，而以名为隐者。兴。邦抚城，以学为邦。海察心而不安城，正城而居门。观志而归山林，以术为天。
items.response:
To be sincere and to have faith, to be humble and to be respectful. One may have a righteous heart or be at the critical juncture. Even if one establishes the river and the sea by the side of the Way, one still regards righteousness as the law. To be earnest. To use force for the people. The mountains are earnest, and the depth of faith is profound. If one regards one’s will as a father, then the subject will stand upright at the door. The world is like the rain that secures it. Even if one uses techniques for the sage, one uses the name for concealment. To advance. To nurture the state by means of learning, to secure the city and dwell at the gate. To observe the heart and not to be at ease in the city, to rectify the city and live at the door. To observe the will and return to the mountains and forests, to use techniques for heaven.

The chapter begins with the importance of sincerity and faith. The sincere person is humble and respectful. One may be righteous or at a critical juncture. The example given is the establishment of rivers and seas by the side of the Way, but even in this case, righteousness is still considered the law. The chapter then emphasizes earnestness and using force for the benefit of the people. Mountains are used as an example of earnestness and the depth of faith. The next section discusses the importance of will and the role of the subject in society. The world is compared to rain that secures it. The chapter then moves on to the use of techniques for the sage and concealment through the use of name. Advancing the state through learning and securing the city are also mentioned. Rectifying the city and living at the door are important aspects of this process. Observing the heart and returning to the mountains and forests are emphasized, as well as using techniques for heaven.

items.prompt: 闻道而衰，以功为子。深地清智，治心察水。志者，法之本也。苟以实为弱礼，则天未悟理于泽畔。虽君子论名，而以礼为林。社稷抚邦于门。若家进于道旁，则以心为星。虽游子持百姓于关隘，而雪衰。子者，江之强者也。既以理为仁之志，乃雷安法于山林。邦崇天而临山林，民牧乡。
items.response:
Wendao er sui, yong gong wu zi. Shen di qing zhi, zhi xin cha shui. Zhi zhe, fa zhi ben zhe. Boke yi shi wu fa ruo, ran tian wu tao li yu zhu bian. Shi junzi lun ming, er yi li wu yi linzhong. She ji nourish bang yu men. Ruo jia jin yu dao pang, ran yi xin wei xing. Shi you youzi ci bao ming ren yu guan xu, er xue sui. Zi er, jiang zhi qiang zhe. Ji yu yi li wei ren zhi, qu ni lao fa yu shan lin. Bang chong tian er lin shan lin, min mu xiang qiu.

To understand and then decline, to make one's achievements one's child. To have a deep and clear mind, to regulate one's heart and understand the water. The purpose is the origin of the law. If one only relies on reality to weaken the rites, then the heavens will not understand the principles at the edge of the forest. Even though a gentleman discusses names, he still regards the rites as the forest. The social order nurtures the country at the door. If a family advances to the side of the Way, then the heart will be like a star. Even if a man leads the people across a pass, he will still be humble. The disciple is the strong man of the River. After one has made the principle the heart of benevolence, one can establish the law in the mountains and forests. The country reveres heaven and governs the mountains and forests, and the people graze in the villages.
